observations.managementAction: Management did not prioritize and still has not established a proper storage area.

observations.recommendation:
1.9 We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. The Municipal Treasurer conduct cash count at year-end for all
accountable officers and provide copies of cash count sheets to the
Municipal Accounting Office to reconcile their records and ensure
the accuracy and completeness of cash in treasury account in the
SFP; and
b. The Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting journal
entry to accurately reflect the Cash – Local Treasury balance,
supported by a summary of the reconciling items.

2.9 We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. The Municipal Accountant prepare an adjusting journal entry at
every year-end for any unreleased/unclaimed checks issued
restoring the Cash in Bank – Local Currency, Current Account, and
the recognition/restoration of liabilities account to faithfully present
the statement of financial position of the Municipality as at
December 31 of any given year.
b. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer properly
coordinate for the list of unreleased checks as of the cutoff date to
facilitate accurate preparation of journal entry vouchers and ensure
proper recording of the same.

3.8 We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. The Municipal Accountant together with the Municipal Engineer and
the Designated General Services Office reconcile their records and
identify the completed infrastructure assets that remained under the
CIP account;
b. The Municipal Accountant provide the necessary adjusting records
to reclassify the completed projects and provide appropriate
depreciation charges’ and
c. The Municipal Accountant disclose in the Notes to FS any
movements of CIP and PPE to ensure accuracy and fairness in the
presentation of the FS.

4.6 We recommended and Management agreed that:
1. The Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries
to record the Sports Equipment and Disaster Response and Rescue
Equipment owned by the Municipality, in consonance with Sections
111 and 112 of Presidential Decree 1445 and International Public
Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) No. 1 and;
2. The Municipal Agriculturist verify, account, and establish the fair
market value of the reported Animals totaling ₱642,000.00 and
coordinate with the Department of Agriculture for proper valuation,
if necessary. Additionally, the report should be submitted to the
Municipal Accountant for recording.

5.7 We recommended and Management agreed that:
1. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer hasten the
reconciliation of Cash in Bank records to bring the account to its
correct amount. Thereafter, conduct reconciliation monthly.
2. The Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant have a
uniform application of the standards in recognizing and recording
cash transactions.

6.14 We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. The Municipal Accountant conduct regular monitoring and review of
accounts to evaluate the current collectability status and provide for
an allowance for impairment of receivables;
b. The Municipal Accountant send demand letters to debtors/ former
accountable officers with shortages to intensify collection efforts
and adopt legal remedies, if warranted;
c. The Office of the Mayor implement strategies to implement the
proper disposition of dormant receivables in strict compliance with
the procedures and requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005, as
amended by COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023;
d. The Municipal Engineer conduct a thorough study in the planning,
and preparation of the program of works, estimates, and other
preparatory documents of proposed projects to be undertaken by
the implementing barangays with careful consideration of the
estimated costs to ensure its prompt and complete implementation;
and
e. The Municipal Accountant together with other concerned offices
incorporate in the MOA the procedures related to monitoring and
evaluation and penalty clauses in case of non-implementation to
ensure timely liquidation of the funds transferred.

7.8 The Municipal Accounting Office and the General Services Office in
coordination with the members of the Inventory Committee fast-track the
reconciliation of the PPE accounts in accordance with COA Circular No.
2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 and ensure the immediate and regular
reconciliation of records of both offices pertaining to the Municipality’s PPEs; and

7.9 The Office of the Mayor implement strategies to ensure that the Office of
the GSO is duly complemented with the needed personnel to fully
function and properly conduct the annual inventory of PPE of the
Municipality instead of the Municipal Accounting Office to comply with
the principles of segregation of duties and internal control.

8.9 We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. The Municipal Assessor’s Office with the Municipal Accountant
conduct immediate and proper validation of real properties based
on the inventory report of the Assessor’s Office and establish actual
ownership of the land, and that future economic benefits will flow to
the Municipality in line with IPSAS 17;
b. The Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries
to record all lands owned by the Municipality, in consonance with
Section 111 of Presidential Decree 1445 and International Public
Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) No. 1; and
c. The Municipal Accountant prepare and maintain a Real Property
Ledger Cards with complete details as to its description, location,
acquisition cost, additions and disposition that will serve as a
subsidiary ledger to support the balances reported in the SFP and
cross balance with the report of the assessor’s office.

9.9 We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. The Municipal Accountant provide disclosures in the Notes to
Financial Statements for CY 2024, the required details/information
for the Property, Plant and Equipment, including total Road Network
System in accordance with IPSAS 17 provisions and COA Circular
No. 2016-004 dated September 30, 2016.
b. The Municipal Engineer substantiate and provide accurate details of
the ₱175,653,500.00 Municipal roads and submits the necessary
documentation to the COA Office and Municipal Accountant.

10.7 We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. The Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting journal entries to
rectify the misclassifications of financial liabilities and ensure
proper review of the recorded transactions per account in
accordance with Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-09 or the
Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs dated December 1, 2015; and
b. The concerned offices make a coordinated effort amongst the
offices in the Municipality and review the charges to accounts
payable, after which derecognize those that are not valid claims to
ensure that only those valid financial liabilities are recognized as
accounts payable.

11.9 We recommended and Management agreed that:
1. The assigned personnel fast-track the reconciliation of the premium
contributions and loan accounts of the officials and employees of
the Municipality and the payment of back premiums owed to GSIS
to decrease the amount reported in the Due to GSIS account;
2. The Human Resources and Management Officer and the Agency
Authorized Officer facilitate the update of any changes in the
Municipality’s membership records promptly and transmit to GSIS
within the month the information (i.e., newly hired employee, new
salary, updated personnel information, etc.) becomes available as
prescribed by Section 6 of RA No. 8291 and GSIS Memorandum
Circular No. 027 series of 2021;
3. The Human Resources and Management Officer continue to require
the monthly deduction from the monthly compensation of
employees with adequate net take-home pay for the loan
amortizations due to GSIS and remit the same in accordance with
Sections 14.2 and 14.3 of RA No. 8291 to avoid the occurrence of
delinquent loan repayments from members; and
4. The employees/personnel listed with delinquent loan repayments
coordinate with GSIS for the reconciliation of their loan accounts
and request for loan restructuring if warranted.

12.18 We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. The HRMO re-assess its existing Organizational Structure and
formulate a Staffing Pattern Plan in accordance with the 2022
Guidelines and Standards in the Establishment of OSSPs so that the
vacant Municipal Head positions could be funded and filled in,
thereby, minimizing the number of hired Job Order employees and
utilizing the existing Job Order employees to fill in the remaining
vacant positions. Also, give importance to the issuance of the
identification card of the JO personnel implying their employment
in the LGU; and
b. The HRMO develop procedural guidelines or policy on hiring job
order workers who are maintenance personnel allowed to work for
5 days to reinforce, clarify standard operating procedure and
establish the need despite current manpower complement of the
Municipality and the requesting Barangays to ensure transparency
and accountability of the government’s scarce resources.
c. The HRMO ensure the adherence to the provisions of Section 76 of
RA 7160 and relevant provisions under CSC No. 12, s. 2022 dated
November 8, 2022, and Memorandum Circular No. 40, series of 1998,
as amended.

13.6 We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. The HRMO make a representation with the BIR Examiner assigned
in the Municipality for a possibility of having a scheduled remote
registration to be held in the Municipality, considering the numbers
and for the ease of Job Order personnel coming from different
Barangays;
b. The HRMO to facilitate an orientation to all the Job Orders regarding
the requirements to be accomplished by them to the BIR; and
c. The Office of the Mayor ensure the adherence to the provisions of
RMC No. 51-2018 dated June 8, 2018, and RMC No. 14-2024 dated
January 24, 2024.

14.8 We recommended and Management agreed that the concerned offices
follow a systematic process of review, especially on the charging of
expenses to refrain from utilizing the LDRRMF for non-disaster-related
programs, projects, and/or activities in conformity with Section 5 of
NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 and Section 4.3 of NDRRMC-DILG-
DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1 to ensure proper fund utilization, accurate
reporting and attain the purpose for which the fund was intended.

15.9 We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant reconcile the Trust–Liability DRRM account and prepare the
corresponding adjusting journal entries by updating the General Ledger
/ Subsidiary Ledger for the transactions related to Trust–Liability DRRM
and record transactions pertaining to the utilization and fund transfers
for all unexpended/unobligated balance made during CY 2019-2023 of the
QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE to reflect the correct amount in the Statement
of Financial Position.

16.7 We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipality transfer
the ₱2,640,507.47 to the Special Trust Fund (STF) to ensure the
implementation of Programs, Projects, and Activities (PPAs) outlined in
the LDRRM Plan. Looking forward, proper accounting of unexpended
LDRRMF should be consistently observed to accurately determine the
amount for transfer to the STF.

17.7 We recommended and Management agreed that:
1. The GFPS establish a GAD Agenda with a consultation with local
functionaries and stakeholders at the beginning of the term of
leadership of the Local Chief Executive.
2. The GFPS conduct Gender Analysis/Assessment during the
formulation of PPAs and re-evaluate the target beneficiaries using
the sex-disaggregated data/information to ensure that Gender
issues/gaps were addressed.

18.6 We recommended and Management agreed that:
1. The Municipal Mayor’s Office submit justifications or explanations
for the abovementioned deficiencies and observations;
2. The BAC prohibit the Purchasing Department to request goods with
reference to brand names or specifications that would lead to a
certain brand. Instead, specifications should have to be the
reference;
3. The Office of the Mayor submit a request for Technical Inspection
for verification and validation of the actual unit and its
specifications; and
4. The Office of the Mayor ensure consistency and accuracy of the
specifications during the procurement process.

19.10 We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. The Municipal Accountant properly review and certified correct the
box F of the DV with regards the accounting entries of a particular
transaction;
b. The Municipal Accountant ensure proper classification of expenses
in accordance with Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-09 or the
Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs to fairly present the accounts
presented in the statement of financial performance as at year-end;
c. The Municipal Accountant submit the lacking documents
enumerated which will be evaluated by the audit team as to
accuracy, propriety, and validity; and
d. The Office of the Mayor create a policy in line with the provisions of
COA Circular 2012-001 and Budget Circular No. 2007-001 to allow a
standard interpretation and application of the grant of honoraria to
the LGU’s service providers to make certain that all honoraria claim
in the future transactions of the LGU are properly recorded,
documented and computed by the provisions of law.

20.10 We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. The Office of the Mayor formulate and adopt internal rules and
regulations pertaining to the rendition of overtime services based
on the CSC – DBM Joint Circular No. 02, s. 2015 dated November 25,
2015, and submit the same to DBM and CSC. Likewise, submit the
“Report on Overtime Services with Pay” to DBM’s Budget and
Management Bureau or Regional Office concerned, copy furnished
to the CSC, upon payment of overtime;
b. The Municipal Budget Officer ensure that during the preparation of
the Annual Budget, Overtime Pay under Personal Services (PS) is
allocated with a budget subject to PS limitation;
c. The Municipal Accountant ensure that the disbursements of
overtime are properly reviewed, covered with proper and complete
documentation, and ascertain that daily time records properly
reflect the time of arrival and departure; and
d. The HRMO revisit the need for a more updated and effective
biometric to safeguard the integrity of data and enhance efficiency
in recording the time logged in and out by the employees.

21.6 We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. The Municipal employees attach the necessary supporting
documents to support the claim of absence in the DTR;
b. The HRMO ensure that the DTR has complete data with a
corresponding signature in case of manual entry;
c. The HRMO ensure that the deductions for late and undertimes be
deducted in the month incurred;
d. The HRMO guarantee that each DTR for Job Order personnel should
be reviewed and properly stamped to warrant that it has been
reviewed by the Office;
e. The requesting personnel/department ensure that all the supporting
documents such as the Purchase Order, Request for Quotation, and
Abstract of Bids are completely filled up and signed by all the
responsible personnel;
f. The Municipal Mayor’s Office prioritize the formation of a functioning
General Services Office team to comply with the submission of the
required Monthly Report for Official Travels per vehicle and the
consolidated Monthly Reports on Fuel Consumption of government
vehicles along with its complete supporting documents for the audit
team to review the reasonableness of fuel consumed during the
period; and
g. The BAC submit the noted lacking documents as per attached Annex
A as a result of the contract review of the six (6) Infrastructure projects
totaling ₱27,782,541.96.

observations.recommendation:
1.7 	We recommended that:
The Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjusting entries in the Cash in Bank Account;
Regularly prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statement in order to present reliable Cash in Bank balance; and
The Mayor’s Office staff timely furnish the Municipal Accountant with the communications relevant to Fund Transfers.

2.5	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the appropriate adjusting entries to reclassify the affected PPE in accordance with COA Circular 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022 with due consideration of the issuance of the Inventory Custodianship Slip to establish accountability of the end-user.

3.9 	We reiterated our recommendation to the Management that:
The Municipal Accountant maintain the required subsidiary ledger, after which draw an adjusting journal entry to recognize the understatement representing each RPT Receivable and SET Receivable account of ₱33,764,705.70 and ₱27,126,230.19, respectively, for calendar year 2023 and tax due from delinquent accounts of previous years, and credit to Deferred Real Property Tax Income and Deferred Special Education Tax Income;
The Municipal Accountant disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements the real property tax delinquent accounts as of December 31, 2023; and
The Municipal Treasurer provide the certified list showing the name of the taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year to the Municipal Accountant for the setting-up of the Real Property Tax Receivable and Special Education Tax Receivable at the beginning of any calendar year.

4.6	We recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office and the General Services Office in coordination with the members of the Inventory Committee fast-track the reconciliation of the PPE accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 and ensure the immediate and regular reconciliation of records of both offices pertaining to the Municipality’s PPEs.

5.8	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, together with the Municipal Assessor, reconcile their records to make the necessary adjusting entries to record all land owned by the Municipality, in consonance with Section 111 of Presidential Decree 1445 and International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) No. 1 and 17.

7.8	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Review the contents of the outstanding checks and coordinate with the Municipal Treasury that they will be furnished with the list of checks staled and cancelled or checks staled but reissued together with the original disbursement vouchers and other supporting documents to facilitate accurate preparation of journal entry vouchers and ensure proper recording of the same.
Submit the Bank Reconciliation Statements for the month of December 2023 in accordance with COA Circular No. 96-011 for verification.
8.6	We recommended that Management speed up the process of closing the dormant bank accounts and revert the same to the source agency and appropriate funds to the Bureau of the Treasury.
9.6	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer hasten the reconciliation of Cash in Bank records to bring the account to its correct amount.

10.7 	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant provide disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements for CY 2023 the required details/information for the Property, Plant and Equipment including Total Road Network System in accordance with IPSAS provisions and COA Circular No. 2016-004 dated September 30, 2016.

11.7	We recommended that the Municipal Engineering Office:
Direct the concerned officers to adhere to the provisions of Appendix 1 of the IRR of RA 9184 by updating their database of all unemployed or underemployed members of the community who are interested in joining Pakyaw groups being formed for the project and by requiring potential pakyaw groups to submit a list of their local community members before submitting their quotations; and
Attach an inspection and acceptance report duly signed by the Municipal Engineer as a supporting reference when paying pakyaw claims to corroborate the accomplishment of work of the laborers and ascertain that the specific work or service output was implemented as planned.

12.13	 We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Require the Municipal Development Council to revisit the local development plan, coordinate, monitor, and evaluate the implementation of development programs and projects to identify priority projects to be embodied in the Annual Investment Plan; and

Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to strictly monitor the     remittance of collection daily. Impose disciplinary actions to those committing the same violation of the regulation.
Assign additional employee/s in the Treasury Office to help speed up collections and remittances.
The Management commented that the Treasury Office would exert more effort in the timely remittance of collections.

We recommended that Management direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to ensure that the financial documents are submitted in accordance to Section 7.2.1(a) of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009. Monitor and follow-up submission of documents by the Municipal Treasurer’s Office.
The Management commented that they would comply with the recommendation. Furthermore, the Municipal Treasurer’s Office would transmit weekly to the Accounting Office.

We recommended to Management the following:
Expedite the processing of the abovementioned lacking  documents so that the interest of the Municipality will be protected.
Direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to thoroughly review the supporting documents in all transactions before certifying the  completeness and propriety thereof.
The Management commented that they would comply with the recommendations. Furthermore, they would discuss the recommendations with the Municipal Assessor.

We recommended to Management the following:
Withhold the salary of the Officials and Personnel with unliquidated Cash Advances, requiring them to fully liquidate their balances.
Prohibit the granting of additional cash advances to Officials and Personnel with existing cash advances to avoid accumulation of unliquidated cash advances.
The Management commented that they will strictly adhere to the recommendations.

We recommended to Management the following:
Require the 44 JOs to refund the CY 2016 Year-end Bonus the amount of ₱ 5,784.45 each , totaling ₱ 254,515.80.
Stop the granting of Year-end Bonus to JOs.
The Management commented that the  amount received by the 44 JOs were unreceived wages from previous months.
The Audit Team would stand that the amount received by the 44 JOs was indeed a bonus. Verification disclosed that there were no unpaid wages for the said JOs.
